# Python 3.11+
"""Domain model for the thread-safe leaderboard.

This file holds the "nouns" of the system: the value objects the public API
hands back, the single exception family, and the sorted index that makes
rank / top-K cheap. There are no locks in here on purpose — concurrency is a
separate concern that lives in the service layer (clean core in §6, thread-safe
subclass in §9).
"""

from bisect import insort, bisect_left
from dataclasses import dataclass


# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# One base exception for the whole domain. Every failure mode we raise OURSELVES
# is a subclass of this. Callers can `except LeaderboardError` to catch anything
# from this system, or catch the specific subclass when they care about one case.
# 🎙️ "All my errors share one base, so a caller can catch the whole family with
#     one except, or a single failure mode when they want to be precise."
class LeaderboardError(Exception):
    """Base class for every error this leaderboard raises."""


class UnknownPlayerError(LeaderboardError):
    """Raised when a rank is requested for a player that was never scored."""


class InvalidScoreError(LeaderboardError):
    """Raised when an increment/score value is not a positive integer."""


class InvalidPlayerError(LeaderboardError):
    """Raised when a player id is empty or not a string."""


class InvalidTopKError(LeaderboardError):
    """Raised when top-K is asked for a non-positive k."""


# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# A plain, never-changing record of one player's standing. We hand this back
# from top_k() so the caller gets a typed object (player_id, score, rank) instead
# of a loose tuple they have to remember the order of.
# 🎙️ "This is just a frozen value object — a snapshot of one row of the board.
#     It never changes after I build it, so it's safe to share across threads."
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Standing:
    player_id: str
    score: int
    rank: int  # 1-based: the best player is rank 1


# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# This is the heart of the read side. It keeps every (score, player) pair in
# ONE list sorted in leaderboard order — highest score first, and within an
# equal score the player whose id sorts earlier comes first (a stable, total
# tie-break). Because the list is always sorted, rank is a binary search
# (O(log n)) and top-K is just the first K entries (O(K)).
#
# The trick that makes binary search work for "highest first" is to store the
# NEGATED score as the sort key. Python sorts ascending; negating the score
# flips it so the largest real score sits at the front. The player id is the
# second key so equal scores still have ONE definite order — no ambiguity about
# who is rank 3 vs rank 4 when they're tied.
# 🎙️ "I keep one sorted list of (negated-score, player-id). Sorted means rank is
#     a binary search and top-K is just a slice off the front. I negate the score
#     so the biggest score sorts to the front, and I break ties on player id so
#     two equal scores still have one definite order."
class SortedBoard:
    """A sorted index of (negated_score, player_id) for O(log n) rank / top-K."""

    def __init__(self) -> None:
        # WHY: the list is the single source of truth for ORDER. The service also
        #      keeps a player -> score dict for O(1) "what is this player's score",
        #      but ORDER lives here so we never re-sort on every read.
        self._keys: list[tuple[int, str]] = []

    @staticmethod
    def _key(player_id: str, score: int) -> tuple[int, str]:
        # WHY: negate the score so a HIGHER score gives a SMALLER key, which sorts
        #      to the front. player_id second makes the order total (no ties left).
        return (-score, player_id)

    def add(self, player_id: str, score: int) -> None:
        # WHY: insort keeps the list sorted in one O(n) splice (the search part is
        #      O(log n)). We never call .sort() on the whole list — that would be
        #      O(n log n) on every write.
        insort(self._keys, self._key(player_id, score))

    def remove(self, player_id: str, score: int) -> None:
        # WHY: when a score changes we delete the OLD (score, player) key before we
        #      insert the new one — otherwise the same player would appear twice.
        key = self._key(player_id, score)
        idx = bisect_left(self._keys, key)

        if idx < len(self._keys) and self._keys[idx] == key:
            self._keys.pop(idx)
            return

        # WHY: a remove that finds nothing means our two structures drifted apart.
        #      That is a programming bug, not a user error, so we surface it loudly
        #      with our own typed error rather than silently ignoring it.
        raise UnknownPlayerError(
            f"cannot remove ({player_id!r}, {score}) — not present in the sorted index"
        )

    def rank_of(self, player_id: str, score: int) -> int:
        # WHY: rank = how many entries sort BEFORE this one, plus one (1-based).
        #      bisect_left gives exactly that count in O(log n) because the list
        #      is sorted and the key is unique per player.
        return bisect_left(self._keys, self._key(player_id, score)) + 1

    def top(self, k: int) -> list[tuple[str, int]]:
        # WHY: the first k entries are already the top k in order. We undo the
        #      negation so the caller sees the real (positive) score.
        out: list[tuple[str, int]] = []

        for neg_score, player_id in self._keys[:k]:
            out.append((player_id, -neg_score))

        return out

    def __len__(self) -> int:
        return len(self._keys)

# Python 3.11+
"""The leaderboard orchestrator.

`LeaderboardService` is the clean, single-threaded-correct core: it owns the
player -> score map and the sorted index, and exposes submit / increment /
rank / top_k. It has TWO no-op concurrency hooks — `_read_lock()` and
`_write_lock()` — that do nothing here. The thread-safe subclass at the bottom
overrides ONLY those two hooks with a real read-write lock, so the core logic
above never changes.

The distinct concurrency angle for this problem is a READER-WRITER lock: rank
and top_k take a SHARED read lock (many readers run in parallel), while submit
and increment take the EXCLUSIVE write lock (one writer, no readers, so an
increment is never lost and a read never sees a half-updated index).
"""

import threading
from collections.abc import Iterator
from contextlib import contextmanager

from models import (
    InvalidPlayerError,
    InvalidScoreError,
    InvalidTopKError,
    SortedBoard,
    Standing,
    UnknownPlayerError,
)


# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# A reader-writer lock (a.k.a. shared/exclusive lock). Many threads may hold the
# READ side at once (they only look, they don't change anything). The WRITE side
# is exclusive: a writer waits until every reader has left, and while it holds
# the lock no reader may enter. This is the right tool here because the workload
# is read-heavy (lots of rank / top-K) with occasional writes (score updates).
#
# We make it WRITER-PREFERRING: once a writer is waiting, new readers queue
# behind it. Without that, a steady stream of readers could starve a writer
# forever (the writer never sees the reader count hit zero).
# 🎙️ "It's a read-write lock. Readers share — any number can read at once. A
#     writer is exclusive — it waits for all readers to leave, then locks
#     everyone out. I make it writer-preferring so a flood of reads can't starve
#     an update."
class ReadWriteLock:
    """Shared lock for readers, exclusive for writers; writer-preferring."""

    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self._cond = threading.Condition(threading.Lock())
        self._readers = 0          # how many readers are inside right now
        self._writer_active = False  # is a writer inside right now
        self._writers_waiting = 0    # writers blocked at the door (for preference)

    @contextmanager
    def read(self) -> Iterator[None]:
        with self._cond:
            # WHY: a reader waits while a writer is inside OR a writer is queued.
            #      The second clause is what makes us writer-preferring — readers
            #      yield to a waiting writer instead of starving it.
            while self._writer_active or self._writers_waiting > 0:
                self._cond.wait()

            self._readers += 1

        try:
            yield
        finally:
            # WHY: release on ANY exit path — even if the caller's body raised —
            #      so a reader can never leak its count and freeze every writer.
            with self._cond:
                self._readers -= 1

                if self._readers == 0:
                    self._cond.notify_all()

    @contextmanager
    def write(self) -> Iterator[None]:
        with self._cond:
            self._writers_waiting += 1

            # WHY: a writer waits until no other writer is active AND no readers
            #      remain — that's what "exclusive" means.
            while self._writer_active or self._readers > 0:
                self._cond.wait()

            self._writers_waiting -= 1
            self._writer_active = True

        try:
            yield
        finally:
            # WHY: clear the flag and wake EVERYONE (waiting writers and readers)
            #      so whoever is next per our preference rule proceeds.
            with self._cond:
                self._writer_active = False
                self._cond.notify_all()


class LeaderboardService:
    """Clean single-threaded core: submit / increment / rank / top_k.

    No real locks here. The two `_read_lock` / `_write_lock` hooks are no-ops;
    the thread-safe subclass overrides them. Read §6 as the pure design and §9
    as the concurrency hardening.
    """

    def __init__(self) -> None:
        # WHY: player -> current score gives O(1) "what is X's score". This is the
        #      authority on VALUE. Order is NOT derivable from a dict, so we keep a
        #      second structure for it.
        self._scores: dict[str, int] = {}

        # WHY: the sorted index owns ORDER. Keeping value and order in two
        #      structures means each read hits the structure built for it — O(1)
        #      score lookup, O(log n) rank, O(K) top-K — with no re-sorting.
        self._board: SortedBoard = SortedBoard()

    # ── concurrency hooks (no-ops in the clean core) ─────────────────────────
    # WHY: these do nothing here, so the core file has zero lock behaviour and
    #      reads like plain single-threaded code. The thread-safe subclass
    #      replaces ONLY these two methods. The diff between "clean" and
    #      "thread-safe" is exactly these hooks — nothing in the logic moves.
    @contextmanager
    def _read_lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
        yield

    @contextmanager
    def _write_lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
        yield

    # ── writes (exclusive) ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
    def submit_score(self, player_id: str, score: int) -> int:
        """Set a player's score to an absolute value; returns the new score."""
        self._validate_player(player_id)
        self._validate_score(score)

        with self._write_lock():
            new_score = self._set_locked(player_id, score)

        return new_score

    def increment_score(self, player_id: str, delta: int) -> int:
        """Add `delta` to a player's score (creating them at 0 first); returns new score."""
        self._validate_player(player_id)
        self._validate_score(delta)

        with self._write_lock():
            # WHY: read-modify-write of the score happens INSIDE the write lock.
            #      This is the whole game: if the read and the write weren't one
            #      exclusive step, two concurrent +1s could both read 5 and both
            #      write 6 — a lost update. The exclusive lock makes it 5 -> 7.
            current = self._scores.get(player_id, 0)
            # WHY: this hook is a no-op in production. It exists so a TEST can widen
            #      the read-modify-write window (force a thread to pause between the
            #      read and the write). Under CPython's GIL pure dict ops never yield
            #      mid-RMW, so without a forced pause the race is invisible — a
            #      missing lock would still pass. The hook lets the suite PROVE the
            #      lock is load-bearing: with the pause, the locked service still
            #      lands seed+W while a no-lock control drops updates.
            self._rmw_window_hook(player_id)
            new_score = self._set_locked(player_id, current + delta)

        return new_score

    def _rmw_window_hook(self, player_id: str) -> None:
        # WHY: production no-op. Overridden ONLY in tests to interleave the
        #      read-modify-write so a missing lock demonstrably loses updates.
        return None

    def _set_locked(self, player_id: str, new_score: int) -> int:
        # WHY: caller already holds the write lock. We pull the old score, remove
        #      its key from the sorted index, then insert the new key. Removing
        #      the OLD key first is what stops a player appearing twice in the
        #      order after their score moves.
        old_score = self._scores.get(player_id)

        if old_score is not None:
            self._board.remove(player_id, old_score)

        self._scores[player_id] = new_score
        self._board.add(player_id, new_score)
        return new_score

    # ── reads (shared) ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    def get_rank(self, player_id: str) -> int:
        """1-based rank of a player (rank 1 = highest score)."""
        self._validate_player(player_id)

        with self._read_lock():
            score = self._scores.get(player_id)

            if score is None:
                # WHY: asking the rank of someone who never scored is a caller
                #      error with a clear typed signal — never a silent -1 or 0.
                raise UnknownPlayerError(f"no score recorded for player {player_id!r}")

            return self._board.rank_of(player_id, score)

    def get_score(self, player_id: str) -> int:
        """Current score of a player."""
        self._validate_player(player_id)

        with self._read_lock():
            score = self._scores.get(player_id)

            if score is None:
                raise UnknownPlayerError(f"no score recorded for player {player_id!r}")

            return score

    def top_k(self, k: int) -> list[Standing]:
        """The top k players as ranked Standings (best first). Fewer if board is smaller."""
        if not isinstance(k, int) or isinstance(k, bool) or k <= 0:
            raise InvalidTopKError(f"k must be a positive int, got {k!r}")

        with self._read_lock():
            # WHY: the first k entries of the sorted index ARE the top k in order,
            #      so rank is just the slice position + 1. No sorting on read.
            pairs = self._board.top(k)
            return [
                Standing(player_id=pid, score=sc, rank=i + 1)
                for i, (pid, sc) in enumerate(pairs)
            ]

    def player_count(self) -> int:
        """How many distinct players have a score (read-only)."""
        with self._read_lock():
            return len(self._scores)

    # ── validation (no lock needed — pure, no shared state) ──────────────────
    @staticmethod
    def _validate_player(player_id: str) -> None:
        # WHY: validate at the public boundary BEFORE we touch any shared state,
        #      so a bad id can never half-mutate the board.
        if not isinstance(player_id, str) or player_id == "":
            raise InvalidPlayerError(f"player_id must be a non-empty string, got {player_id!r}")

    @staticmethod
    def _validate_score(value: int) -> None:
        # WHY: scores/increments must be positive ints. bool is an int subclass in
        #      Python, so we reject it explicitly — True sneaking in as 1 would be
        #      a silent data bug.
        if not isinstance(value, int) or isinstance(value, bool) or value <= 0:
            raise InvalidScoreError(f"score/delta must be a positive int, got {value!r}")


# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# The thread-safe version. It changes NOTHING about the logic — it only swaps
# the two no-op hooks for a real read-write lock. Reads take the shared side;
# writes take the exclusive side. Because the core methods already call
# `_read_lock()` / `_write_lock()` around exactly the right blocks, wrapping
# them in a real lock is all it takes to make the whole service correct under
# many concurrent readers and writers.
# 🎙️ "The thread-safe version is a subclass that overrides only the two lock
#     hooks. Reads share, writes are exclusive. The core logic file doesn't
#     change by a single line — that's the whole point of the no-op hook."
class ThreadSafeLeaderboardService(LeaderboardService):
    """Same logic as the core, but the two hooks now use a real read-write lock."""

    def __init__(self) -> None:
        super().__init__()
        self._rw = ReadWriteLock()

    @contextmanager
    def _read_lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
        with self._rw.read():
            yield

    @contextmanager
    def _write_lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
        with self._rw.write():
            yield

# Python 3.11+
"""Demo driver for the thread-safe leaderboard.

Run with: python main.py
Each case prints a single "Case N ... -> OK" line so a green run is obvious.
Cases cover: submit + rank, increment (lost-update-free), top-K ordering with a
tie-break, the error paths, and a concurrent barrier-synced race that yields the
GIL inside the read-modify-write window (so the race is real, not GIL-masked) and
proves the thread-safe service still never loses an update. The pytest suite adds
a negative control showing the SAME race drops updates without the lock.
"""

import threading
import time

from leaderboard_service import LeaderboardService, ThreadSafeLeaderboardService
from models import (
    InvalidPlayerError,
    InvalidScoreError,
    InvalidTopKError,
    UnknownPlayerError,
)


class _YieldingThreadSafeService(ThreadSafeLeaderboardService):
    """Thread-safe service whose read-modify-write yields the GIL mid-window.

    WHY: pure dict ops never yield under CPython's GIL, so a missing lock would
    still pass a naive concurrency demo — the GIL, not the lock, would be hiding
    the race. Yielding inside the RMW window makes the increment genuinely
    interruptible, so the demo only stays correct BECAUSE the exclusive write
    lock keeps the read-modify-write atomic across the yield.
    """

    def _rmw_window_hook(self, player_id: str) -> None:
        time.sleep(0)


def case_1_submit_and_rank() -> None:
    lb = LeaderboardService()
    lb.submit_score("alice", 100)
    lb.submit_score("bob", 250)
    lb.submit_score("carol", 175)

    # bob (250) is rank 1, carol (175) rank 2, alice (100) rank 3.
    assert lb.get_rank("bob") == 1, "bob should be rank 1"
    assert lb.get_rank("carol") == 2, "carol should be rank 2"
    assert lb.get_rank("alice") == 3, "alice should be rank 3"
    print("Case 1 submit + 1-based rank (highest first) -> OK")


def case_2_increment_no_lost_update() -> None:
    lb = LeaderboardService()
    lb.submit_score("dave", 10)
    assert lb.increment_score("dave", 5) == 15, "10 + 5 should be 15"
    assert lb.increment_score("dave", 5) == 20, "15 + 5 should be 20"
    # A brand-new player increments from an implicit 0.
    assert lb.increment_score("erin", 7) == 7, "new player should start at 0"
    assert lb.get_score("dave") == 20, "dave's stored score should be 20"
    print("Case 2 increment is read-modify-write, new player starts at 0 -> OK")


def case_3_top_k_with_tie_break() -> None:
    lb = LeaderboardService()
    lb.submit_score("zoe", 50)
    lb.submit_score("amy", 50)   # tie with zoe — amy's id sorts first
    lb.submit_score("max", 90)

    top = lb.top_k(2)
    assert [s.player_id for s in top] == ["max", "amy"], "max then amy (tie broken by id)"
    assert [s.rank for s in top] == [1, 2], "ranks should be 1, 2"
    # Asking for more than exist returns everyone, no error.
    assert len(lb.top_k(10)) == 3, "top_k(10) on 3 players returns 3"
    print("Case 3 top-K ordering with a stable id tie-break -> OK")


def case_4_error_paths() -> None:
    lb = LeaderboardService()
    lb.submit_score("ann", 5)

    for bad_call, err in [
        (lambda: lb.get_rank("ghost"), UnknownPlayerError),
        (lambda: lb.get_score("ghost"), UnknownPlayerError),
        (lambda: lb.submit_score("", 5), InvalidPlayerError),
        (lambda: lb.submit_score("ann", 0), InvalidScoreError),
        (lambda: lb.increment_score("ann", -3), InvalidScoreError),
        (lambda: lb.submit_score("ann", True), InvalidScoreError),
        (lambda: lb.top_k(0), InvalidTopKError),
    ]:
        try:
            bad_call()
        except err:
            pass
        else:
            raise AssertionError(f"expected {err.__name__} but nothing was raised")

    print("Case 4 every bad input raises its specific typed error -> OK")


def case_5_concurrent_increments_no_lost_update() -> None:
    # Many writer threads each increment the SAME shared player while reader
    # threads read in parallel. The barrier makes them all hit the service at
    # the same instant — a real race, not threads taking turns. The service
    # yields the GIL inside the read-modify-write window so the race is REAL: a
    # missing lock would drop updates here (the pytest suite has a negative
    # control proving exactly that). The score staying at seed + writers is the
    # proof the exclusive write lock — not the GIL — is doing the work.
    lb = _YieldingThreadSafeService()
    lb.submit_score("shared", 1)  # seed at 1 (submit needs a positive score)

    writers = 50
    read_errors: list[str] = []

    def writer() -> None:
        barrier.wait()
        lb.increment_score("shared", 1)

    def reader() -> None:
        barrier.wait()
        try:
            lb.get_rank("shared")
            lb.top_k(3)
        except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 - test harness records any reader fault
            read_errors.append(repr(exc))

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=writer) for _ in range(writers)]
    threads += [threading.Thread(target=reader) for _ in range(writers)]
    barrier = threading.Barrier(len(threads))
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    # Seed was 1, then 50 writers each added 1 -> exactly 51. A plain non-atomic
    # increment would lose updates and land below 51.
    final = lb.get_score("shared")
    assert final == 1 + writers, f"expected {1 + writers}, got {final} (lost update!)"
    assert not read_errors, f"readers saw faults: {read_errors}"
    print(f"Case 5 {writers} concurrent writers + {writers} readers, no lost update (score={final}) -> OK")


def main() -> None:
    case_1_submit_and_rank()
    case_2_increment_no_lost_update()
    case_3_top_k_with_tie_break()
    case_4_error_paths()
    case_5_concurrent_increments_no_lost_update()
    print("\nAll cases passed.")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

# Python 3.11+
"""Add the workspace root to sys.path so tests can `from models import ...`.

The tests live one directory down; this shim lets them import the flat
sibling modules without a package install.
"""

import os
import sys

sys.path.insert(0, os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))))

# Python 3.11+
"""Pytest suite for the thread-safe leaderboard.

Covers every public method, every typed error path, boundary cases, and the
concurrency race with STRICT post-state assertions (not just a count): final
scores equal the exact sum of increments (no lost update), ranks stay
consistent, and top-K is correct.
"""

import threading
import time

import pytest

from leaderboard_service import LeaderboardService, ThreadSafeLeaderboardService
from models import (
    InvalidPlayerError,
    InvalidScoreError,
    InvalidTopKError,
    Standing,
    UnknownPlayerError,
)


# ── test doubles that widen the read-modify-write window ──────────────────────
# WHY: under CPython's GIL the RMW in increment_score never yields between the
#      score read and the dict write (it's pure in-memory ops), so a missing lock
#      would NOT lose updates and the concurrency test would pass trivially —
#      giving false confidence that the lock is load-bearing. These doubles
#      override the no-op `_rmw_window_hook` to FORCE a pause/interleave inside
#      that window. The locked service must still land seed+W (the lock holds the
#      RMW atomic across the pause); the no-lock control must drop updates (proving
#      the lock — not the GIL — is what prevents the loss).
class _YieldingThreadSafeService(ThreadSafeLeaderboardService):
    """Thread-safe service whose RMW yields the GIL — the lock must still hold."""

    def _rmw_window_hook(self, player_id: str) -> None:
        # WHY: sleep(0) hands the GIL to another thread mid-RMW. Because we hold
        #      the EXCLUSIVE write lock, no other writer can enter and read the
        #      same stale value, so the increment still cannot be lost.
        time.sleep(0)


class _RacyBarrierService(LeaderboardService):
    """No-lock control whose RMW forces every writer to read BEFORE any writes.

    The barrier inside the window makes all writers complete their read of the
    shared score before any of them writes it back. With no lock to serialize
    them, they all read the same stale value and all write `stale + delta`, so
    only ONE increment survives — the textbook lost update.

    We override `_set_locked` to update ONLY the value dict: the lost-update
    invariant is about the stored score, and bypassing the SortedBoard mutation
    keeps the demonstration deterministic (concurrent board pops would otherwise
    raise on a no-lock service and muddy the proof). The point is purely that an
    unlocked read-modify-write drops the score updates the lock would have kept.
    """

    def __init__(self, writers: int) -> None:
        super().__init__()
        self._read_gate = threading.Barrier(writers)

    def _rmw_window_hook(self, player_id: str) -> None:
        # WHY: every writer parks here right after reading `current` and before
        #      writing it back. The barrier releases only once ALL writers have
        #      read, guaranteeing they all saw the same stale value — a
        #      deterministic lost update on an unlocked service.
        self._read_gate.wait()

    def _set_locked(self, player_id: str, new_score: int) -> int:
        # WHY: value-only write so the proof depends solely on the lost-update of
        #      the score, never on concurrent SortedBoard mutation raising.
        self._scores[player_id] = new_score
        return new_score

# ── concurrency: barrier-synced writers + readers, strict post-state ──────────
def test_concurrent_increments_have_no_lost_update() -> None:
    """W writers each increment a shared + a distinct player while R readers read.

    The service used here (`_YieldingThreadSafeService`) yields the GIL *inside*
    the read-modify-write window, so the RMW is genuinely interruptible — a
    missing lock WOULD drop updates (see the negative control test below). This
    proves the reader-writer lock, not the GIL, is what keeps the increment
    atomic.

    Strict assertions:
      - the shared player's final score == exactly the sum of all increments
      - every distinct player's score == its single increment
      - ranks are internally consistent (sorted by score, total order)
      - top_k matches the scores dict exactly
      - no reader ever saw an exception or a half-updated index
    A plain lock that dropped updates would fail the shared-score assertion.
    """
    lb = _YieldingThreadSafeService()
    writers = 64
    lb.submit_score("shared", 1)  # seed at 1 (submit requires a positive score)

    reader_faults: list[str] = []
    rank_observations: list[int] = []
    barrier = threading.Barrier(writers * 2)  # writers + an equal number of readers

    def writer(i: int) -> None:
        barrier.wait()  # all hit the critical section at one instant
        lb.increment_score("shared", 1)
        lb.submit_score(f"solo-{i}", i + 1)  # distinct player, distinct score

    def reader() -> None:
        barrier.wait()
        try:
            rank_observations.append(lb.get_rank("shared"))
            top = lb.top_k(5)
            # ranks within a single top_k snapshot must be 1..len, strictly increasing
            assert [s.rank for s in top] == list(range(1, len(top) + 1))
            # scores within the snapshot must be non-increasing (sorted highest first)
            scores = [s.score for s in top]
            assert scores == sorted(scores, reverse=True)
        except BaseException as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 - record ANY reader fault for the assert below
            reader_faults.append(repr(exc))

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=writer, args=(i,)) for i in range(writers)]
    threads += [threading.Thread(target=reader) for _ in range(writers)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    # 1) NO LOST UPDATE: seed 1 + `writers` increments of 1 == exactly writers + 1.
    assert lb.get_score("shared") == writers + 1

    # 2) every distinct player landed its exact score.
    for i in range(writers):
        assert lb.get_score(f"solo-{i}") == i + 1

    # 3) population is exactly the shared player plus every distinct one.
    assert lb.player_count() == writers + 1

    # 4) no reader ever observed a fault or an inconsistent snapshot.
    assert not reader_faults, reader_faults
    assert all(r >= 1 for r in rank_observations)

    # 5) final ranks are a consistent total order: rank 1 has the max score, and
    #    rank equals position in the fully-sorted board.
    everyone = lb.top_k(writers + 1)
    assert [s.rank for s in everyone] == list(range(1, writers + 2))
    assert everyone[0].score == max(s.score for s in everyone)
    descending = [s.score for s in everyone]
    assert descending == sorted(descending, reverse=True)


def test_negative_control_no_lock_loses_updates() -> None:
    """Negative control: the SAME race on a NO-LOCK service MUST drop updates.

    This is what proves the reader-writer lock is load-bearing. `_RacyBarrierService`
    is the plain (no-lock) core with the identical RMW interleave forced via a
    barrier inside the read-modify-write window: every writer reads the shared
    score before any writer writes it back. Without a lock to serialize them they
    all read the same stale value, so the final score is FAR below seed + writers
    and only one increment survives. If this test ever stopped dropping updates,
    the concurrency test above would be proving nothing.
    """
    writers = 8
    lb = _RacyBarrierService(writers)
    lb.submit_score("shared", 1)  # seed at 1

    def writer() -> None:
        lb.increment_score("shared", 1)

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=writer) for _ in range(writers)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    # All writers read the seed (1) before any wrote, so every write stored 1 + 1
    # = 2: the final score is exactly 2, NOT seed + writers (== 9). The lock is the
    # only thing that would have prevented this — its absence loses 7 of 8 updates.
    final = lb.get_score("shared")
    assert final == 2, f"expected a lost update (final 2), got {final}"
    assert final < 1 + writers, "no-lock service must drop updates under the race"


def test_concurrent_distinct_player_writes_all_land() -> None:
    """Pure write contention on DISTINCT players — every write must survive."""
    lb = ThreadSafeLeaderboardService()
    n = 100
    barrier = threading.Barrier(n)

    def writer(i: int) -> None:
        barrier.wait()
        lb.submit_score(f"p-{i}", (i + 1) * 10)

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=writer, args=(i,)) for i in range(n)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    assert lb.player_count() == n
    # highest score is p-(n-1) with (n)*10, so it must be rank 1
    assert lb.get_rank(f"p-{n - 1}") == 1
    assert lb.get_rank("p-0") == n
    top3 = lb.top_k(3)
    assert [s.player_id for s in top3] == [f"p-{n - 1}", f"p-{n - 2}", f"p-{n - 3}"]
